coredata.dc:title: A new composite of nano zero-valent iron encapsulated in carbon dots for oxidative removal of bio-refractory antibiotics from water
coredata.dc:creator: Pirsaheb, Meghdad
Moradi, Sajad
Shahlaei, Mohsen
Wang, Xiangke
Farhadian, Negin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel Gum Tragacanth-based carbon dots-nano zero-valent iron composite was prepared via cost-effective and green one-step hydrothermal method. A new composite was applied for its potential as a novel heterogeneous Fenton-like catalyst in order to remove bio-refractory antibiotics from aqueous solution. The morphological, structural, electrochemical and physicochemical properties of the new composite were evaluated using TEM, SEM, DLS, Cyclic voltammetry, XRD and FTIR techniques. Development of nano-scale metallic iron particles encapsulated in carbon dot films with an average size of ∼237 nm was observed. Using experimental design and response surface methodology, at optimum process conditions ([H2O2] of 20 mM, time of 60 min and amount of catalyst of 765 mg/l), a superior catalytic performance of the composites were confirmed by removing rate of amoxicillin and ciprofloxacin equal to 90% and 51%, respectively. From the mechanism study using FTIR analysis, it can be concluded that composite reacts with H2O2 and due to oxidation, different iron oxides are produced indicating nano zero-valen iron particles is reduced and convert H2O2 to OH• radicals. The encapsulation of nano zero-valent iron in biopolymer based carbon dots can enhance air/water stability and prolong the reactivity of nano zero-valent iron with H2O2. Results indicate that carbon dots-nano zero-valent iron composite-mediated heterogeneous Fenton oxidation technology is an effective strategy for pollutant abatement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amoxicillin
Ciprofloxacin
Carbon dot
Fenton
Nano zero-valent iron

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Testing the oxidation ability of NZVI@CD particles
Benefits of using GT for green NZVI@CD catalysts synthesize
Oxidative antibiotic removal using catalytic Fenton reaction
Exploring mechanism of the reaction between NZVI@CD and H2O2
Stability test of the NZVI@CD and bare NZVI nanoparticles
Application of Fenton reaction in real wastewater

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Council of Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Deputy for Research and Technology, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Iran National Science Foundation, Iran (Grant Number: 95014379 ) and Research Council of Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Iran (Grant Number: 96349 ) for their financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of the NZVI@CD particles. Apparent peaks were ascribed to graphitic carbon (G), Fe0 (F), FeO (O), γ-Fe2O3 (M) and α-Fe2O3 (H). (b) SEM representation of the NZVI@CD particles. (c), (d) TEM images of the NZVI@CD particles, (e) Cyclic voltammogram for the NZVI@CD particles.
3D surface plots for antibiotic removal using NZVI@CD catalysts indicating interaction between variables.
Perturbation plot showing the effect of variables on AMX removal efficiency.
Graphical plot for the optimized antibiotic removal conditions by desirability approach.
FTIR spectra for the NZVI@CD particles before and after reaction with H2O2.
Average particle size of the bare-NZVI and NZVI@CD nanoparticles during 3 days.
Generation of hydrogen gas due to reaction of nanoparticles with H2O2 during 7 days. 1) bare NZVI, 2) NZVI@CD nanoparticle.
Results of the antibiotic removal efficiencies with different types of nanoparticles.
CCFD experimental design matrix for antibiotic removal.
Comparison of the observed and predicted removal efficiencies.
ANOVA test for the AMX (R1) and CIP (R2) removal efficiencies.
Main parameters of the wastewater form Farabi hospital.

head.ce:title: A new composite of nano zero-valent iron encapsulated in carbon dots for oxidative removal of bio-refractory antibiotics from water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Environmental Determinants of Health (RCEDH), School of Public Health, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Environmental Determinants of Health (RCEDH)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Drug Delivery Research Center, School of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University, Beijing 102206, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Environmental Health Engineering, School of Public Health, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel Gum Tragacanth-based carbon dots-nano zero-valent iron composite was prepared via cost-effective and green one-step hydrothermal method. A new composite was applied for its potential as a novel heterogeneous Fenton-like catalyst in order to remove bio-refractory antibiotics from aqueous solution. The morphological, structural, electrochemical and physicochemical properties of the new composite were evaluated using TEM, SEM, DLS, Cyclic voltammetry, XRD and FTIR techniques. Development of nano-scale metallic iron particles encapsulated in carbon dot films with an average size of ∼237 nm was observed. Using experimental design and response surface methodology, at optimum process conditions ([H2O2] of 20 mM, time of 60 min and amount of catalyst of 765 mg/l), a superior catalytic performance of the composites were confirmed by removing rate of amoxicillin and ciprofloxacin equal to 90% and 51%, respectively. From the mechanism study using FTIR analysis, it can be concluded that composite reacts with H2O2 and due to oxidation, different iron oxides are produced indicating nano zero-valen iron particles is reduced and convert H2O2 to OH• radicals. The encapsulation of nano zero-valent iron in biopolymer based carbon dots can enhance air/water stability and prolong the reactivity of nano zero-valent iron with H2O2. Results indicate that carbon dots-nano zero-valent iron composite-mediated heterogeneous Fenton oxidation technology is an effective strategy for pollutant abatement.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic substances as widely used pharmaceuticals in human societies exhibit anti-bacterial, anti-parasitic and antifungal activities against the living organisms. The wide occurrences of antibiotics which enter the aquatic environments through the disposal of unused/expired as well as the excretion of un-metabolized amounts in the body change various ecosystems. Even low concentrations of them (i.e. nanograms/micrograms per liter) pose risk to humans through spread bacterial drug resistance and failure of treatment with antibiotics. Among various treatment technologies like biodegradation, membrane processes, absorption, nanofiltration, and reverse osmosis, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been appropriate for their treatment. In general, AOPs generate highly reactive free radicals (mainly OH•) to effectively degrade β-lactams and fluoroquinolones. The classic Fenton is a very effective AOPs that including the reaction between ferrous ions and H2O2 under acidic conditions to form the OH•. This process only performed at the limited pH range (2.0–4.0), a high amount of ferrous iron salts are needed and finally, a huge amount of sludge are produced (Goi and Trapido, 2002; Hu et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2015). Recently, with the introduction of zero-valent iron (ZVI) as a source of Fe (II), the Fenton process was further improved (Kallel et al., 2009; Xiong et al., 2015). In this case, after oxidation of ZVI by protons through a two electron transfer, Fe(II) is formed and reacts with H2O2 to generate OH• (Xu and Wang, 2011; Li et al., 2013). Several studies have been devoted to the using ZVI and its nano-scale i.e. nano zero-valent iron (NZVI) form for the treatment of halogenated organic molecules (Song and Carraway, 2005; Zhang et al., 2011a), nitrates (Hao and Zhang, 2017), dyes (Nam and Tratnyek, 2000), nitroaromatic compounds (Bandstra et al., 2005), pesticides (Keum and Li, 2004) and heavy metals (Yoon et al., 2011; Zhu et al., 2018). Despite outstanding benefits like high surface area, fast kinetics, high reactivity and small particle size (Wang et al., 2014), its effectiveness has been affected by some disadvantages. ZVI/NZVI has a high surface energy thus quickly agglomerate and possesses poor air stability and hence quickly oxidizes (Song and Carraway, 2005; Rastogi et al., 2009). In order to improve their stability and better distribution, various types of materials such as activated carbon, alumina, bentonite, kaolinite, zeolite, carbon spheres and carbon polystyrene resin, were applied as a support for ZVI/NZVI (Zhu et al., 2009; Chen et al., 2011; Jiang et al., 2011; Karabelli et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2011b). The liquid-phase reduction method with borohydride salt was applied for the preparation of supported NZVI composites (Zhu et al., 2009; Chen et al., 2011; Karabelli et al., 2011). This method suffers from complex and expensive processing because of vacuum operation, the N2 atmosphere, borohydride cost, and formation of a large amount of hydrogen (Sun et al., 2012). A few studies suggested the encapsulation of NZVI into different materials as a promising way of enhancing stability, suspension, and transportation of it (Sun et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2015; Dai et al., 2016; Sheng et al., 2016). These studies fabricated the encapsulated NZVI by a hydrothermal route followed by a self-reduction in the N2 atmosphere.

In our previous study, Gum Tragacanth-based nano carbon dots (NCDs) were successfully synthesized (Moradi et al., 2018). The efficacies of the as-prepared NCDs were evaluated for catalytic decomposition of hydrogen peroxide and subsequently oxidative degradation of phenol (Pirsaheb et al., 2018). Due to excellent ability of biopolymer based NCDs in the production of hydroxyl radicals and complete phenol oxidation, its effectiveness was further studied. In this regard, Gum Tragacanth-based carbon dots have been applied as a matrix to minimize size effects and aggregation as well as to facilitate the dispersion of NZVI in aqueous solution. For the encapsulation of NZVI, the simple one-pot hydrothermal process under mild conditions was used without utilization of N2 atmosphere and high-cost borohydride. The feasibility of applying NZVI particles encapsulated in carbon dots to enhance heterogeneous catalytic Fenton oxidation of two widely used antibiotics, Amoxicillin (AMX) and Ciprofloxacin (CIP), were investigated. Probing assay was undertaken to identify the prominent role of hydroxyl radicals for antibiotic removal and suggest the effective reactive radical formation during the heterogeneous H2O2 activation by NZVI@CD catalyst. The main advantages of preparation and using NZVI@CD for pollutant removal are summarized as follow:
•
Using Gum Tragacanth (GT), a safe, biodegradable and biocompatible biopolymer to simultaneously reduction, carbonization and encapsulation of NZVI.
•
Applying environmental friendly and low cost one-pot hydrothermal method for NZVI@CD synthesize.
•
Carbon dots from GT act as reducing substance for convert iron salt to Fe0 along with acting as a capping agent to preserve nano Fe0 particles from fast oxidation.
•
Not using toxic and expensive chemicals in NZVI@CD synthesizes.
•
Not using the N2 atmosphere for Fe0 synthesizes.

This work has been conducted to develop new encapsulated NZVI composites by an in situ hydrothermal reduction method without the addition of any chemicals and without the need for a reduction step under the nitrogen atmosphere. This NZVI@CD composites are synthesized using carbothermal reduction of Gum Tragacanth, a green biopolymer, to carbon dots. Besides the ability of carbon dots to iron salt reduction, the enhanced NZVI stability and activity was obtained due to the protective effect of the carbohydrate skeleton capable of encapsulating NZVI. This study focuses on simplifying the procedure and reducing the synthesis cost, and in comparison to the conventional liquid-phase reduction synthesis method in which performed in two individual steps, the reduction and encapsulation occurred simultaneously in one step. This new and low-cost synthesis method facilitate NZVI utilization in large-scale applications.

The raw Gum Tragacanth (GT) was collected from naturally growing plants in different regions of Iran. The GT powder was prepared as mentioned in our previous study (Moradi et al., 2018). An analytical grade of Ciprofloxacin (CIP) and Amoxicillin (AMX) were provided from Sigma Aldrich and used without further purification. All of the chemicals including Ferric chloride, NaOH, HCl, H2SO4, and ethanol were of analytical grade.

In this study, nano zero-valent iron encapsulated in carbon dot (NZVI@CD) was prepared via a green hydrothermal route without adding toxic chemicals. For this purpose, initially, Ferric chloride was dissolved in ethanol. GT powder was dissolved in an ethanol deionized water solution (3:1, v/v) kept at room temperature overnight. In a typical synthesize, the mixture of ferric solution, GT solution (1% w/w) and 5 mL of 5 M NaOH were transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave followed by a hydrothermal reaction in an oven at 180 °C for a period of 3 h. After cooling at room temperature, the black suspension was filtered and washed three times with ethanol. Finally, the black precipitate dried at 80 °C under vacuum oven.

The morphology and particle size of the NZVI@CD particles were studied using scanning and transmission (Shimadzu, Japan) electron microscopy techniques. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) pattern was acquired using Inel Equaniox 3000 (France), 30 kV, 20 mA, with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained by a Shimadzu IR2 spectrometer (Japan) instrument using an ATR mode. The oxidation-reduction potentials of the NZVI@CD particles were analyzed by electro-analytical cyclic voltammetric (CV) technique using a Potentiostat Metrohm μlab III (Switzerland). A conventional three-electrode system was applied which containing an Ag/AgCl/3 M KCl as a reference electrode, platinum wire, and NZVI@CD embedded in the carbon paste as a counter and working electrode, respectively. Cyclic voltammograms were measured with 0.01 M PBS (pH 7.4) at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1. Average particle size distribution and polydispersity index (PDI) were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) by Malvern Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd.Worcestershire, UK). Chemical oxygen demand (COD) and biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) were analyzed according to the Standard Methods. Conductivity and Turbidity were determined using 2510 B. Laboratory Method and 2130 B. Nephelometric Method, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing the oxidation ability of NZVI@CD particles
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the oxidation ability of the synthesized NZVI@CD particles for the removal of AMX and CIP, a series of experiments were performed based on a design of experiment methodology. A central composite face-centered design (CCFD) and RSM were used to examine the effects of three independent variables and identifying the influences of their interactions on the removal efficiency using Design Expert Software (ver. 7). A CCFD is a simpler design and also is able to reduce the prediction error in comparison to other types of CCD which they are depends on more level of setting for each factor and hence leading to a higher possibility of errors related with operation and setup (Zhang and Xiaofeng, 2009; Lam et al., 2017). Process variables (hydrogen peroxide amount, catalyst dosage and reaction time) and their considered ranges are shown in Table 1 . The designed experiments are presented in Table 2 . A face-centered design including a total number of 17 experimental runs which require carrying out a triplicate experiment at a center point and ensure the reproducibility of the data and computing the experimental error (Kundu et al., 2015; Lam et al., 2017).

Experimental runs were performed in a 50 mL batch reactor containing 100 ppm of an antibiotic solution at ambient temperature and pressure and the solution pH was adjusted to approximately 3.5 with sodium hydroxide and sulfuric acid. Based on literature studies, in our experiments, the pH value was set to 3.5. It was reported that when the pH value is less than 3, the Fenton reaction strongly influenced and the amount of OH• reduced. At low pH, due to an oxonium ions H3O2 + formation, the H2O2 is stable and less reductive against ferrous ion (Elmolla and Chaudhuri, 2009 a; b). Moreover, the amount of Fe3+, soluble iron, decreases at low pH and hence suppressing the OH• production. Besides, the scavenging ability of H+ ions affected the production of OH• at low pH (Lucas and Peres, 2006). When pH is greater than 4, because of the iron hydroxides precipitation, the regeneration of the Fe2+ and OH• production mainly inhibited (El-Desoky et al., 2010).

According to Table 2, in each run, a known amount of NZVI@CD particles and a required amount of H2O2 were added to the antibiotic aqueous solution under magnetic stirring continuously. At selected reaction times, aliquots of the solution were withdrawn and followed by quenching the Fenton reaction by adding methanol. The liquid chromatographic analysis (Agilent 1100 Series HPLC) was applied for the measurement of antibiotics concentrations. All experiments were carried out twice and finally, the average value was reported. The standard deviation between repeated measurements was less than 5%. The removal efficiencies of AMX and CIP as two response variables were calculated using the following formula:
(1)
R =
C
0
−
C
f
C
f
where R is the removal efficiency (%) of each antibiotic after catalytic Fenton reaction, C0 and Cf are the initial and the final concentration of antibiotics based on ppm. R1 and R2 represent the removal efficiencies of AMX and CIP, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Benefits of using GT for green NZVI@CD catalysts synthesize
ce:section.ce:para: Using GT as a biocompatible and biodegradable polymer, the NZVI@CD particles are prepared. In addition to the lack of a nitrogen atmosphere, in the preparation procedure, the chemical compound of NaBH4 has not been used. Also, these nanoparticles do not need to create coatings for stabilization, since the resulting carbon dots from GT has a reducing property and in addition to iron reduction and forming Fe0 nanoparticles, can act as a capping agent. In this way, the synthesis and stabilization of the nanoparticles are carried out simultaneously and in one step by a homogeneous substance. This homogeneity in terms of what the final functional groups of the nanostructure are specified and well-known, facilitate their convenient covalent bonding in order to stabilize the catalyst to different surfaces. This synthesis method has a lot of priority than synthesis with plant extracts which are heterogeneous compounds and their superficial functional groups are completely uncertain.

Low angle XRD patterns of the as-synthesized NZVI@CD particles are illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). A peak at (2θ = 25°) belongs to the plane (100) of graphite was observed. Cubic structured γ-Fe2O3 (JCPDS no. 39–1346) was confirmed by the presence of diffraction peaks at 35.9°, 57.6° and 62.7° which corresponding to the (3 1 1), (4 2 2), and (4 4 0) crystal planes, respectively (Fardis et al., 2012). The peak at 2θ = 64.3° could be indexed to (2 0 0) plane of the pure Fe0 (JCPDS 65–4899) structure (Sun et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2015). Due to surface hydroxylation, the main characteristic peak of Fe0 at 44.7° was shifted to the left and the right positions (41.1° and 49.7°). The peaks at (41.1°, 72.1°, and 75.6°) are assigned to iron oxide (FeO) crystalline phases (Dai et al., 2016). Finally, three diffraction peaks at 33.4°, 49.7°, and 54.3° are ascribed to the (1 0 4), (0 2 4) and (1 1 6) planes of the Hematite (α-Fe2O3) (Schrebler et al., 2014). These XRD results confirmed that the NZVI@CD particles consisted basically of metallic Fe0, graphitic carbon, FeO, α- and γ-Fe2O3 structures. DLS technique was used as a useful tool for determining the size and polydispersity of nanoparticles. In order to disperse probable aggregates without destroying nanoparticles structures, after mechanical stirring, the nanoparticles were briefly sonicated. The average particle size distribution of the as-prepared NZVI@CD particles found to be 237 nm. Also, the particles analyzed are well polydisperse with polydispersity index 0.18 which is less than 0.3. The Polydispersity index (PDI) is a dimensionless number which determines the degree of variation of the particle size in ranges between 0 and 1, a low PDI indicates uniformity that results in less Ostwald ripening and hence stability in the system (Farhadian et al., 2018). The size distribution and physical aspects of the NZVI@CD particles were further studied using SEM and TEM images. The SEM image of NZVI@CD particles is shown in Fig. 1(b). The smooth and solid surfaces of the NZVI@CD particles are observed in the SEM image. As can be seen from the TEM images in Fig. 1(c) and (d), the fine black particles were loaded and entered into the film or sheet-like structure domains. The majority of the 200 nm NZVI@CD particles is shown and seems as aggregated structures in the range of 150–200 nm which composed of zero-valent iron coated with carbon dots in the size of 10–40 nm. In comparison, the obtained size of the NZVI@CD particles for DLS technique is higher than that of both SEM and TEM. The formation of aggregates could be considered as a reason for this difference and such aggregates are evident in the SEM image. To understand an electrochemical behavior of the system containing zero-valent iron (ZVI) particles a cyclic voltammetry analysis is carried out. A typical voltammogram is depicted in Fig. 1(e). It is well-known that zero-valent iron (ZVI) particles are an aggressive reducing agent. Thus the addition of NZVI particles into the medium creates intensive reducing conditions. As evident from a cyclic voltammogram, peaks appear at negative potentials and confirm this fact that a rate of reduction in zero-valent iron ion may higher than oxidation. These results further indicated that the NZVI@CD particles show the high electrocatalytic activity for decomposition of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidative antibiotic removal using catalytic Fenton reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
The NZVI@CD particles as an eco-friendly and novel practically acceptable catalyst were used to generation of hydroxyl radicals from H2O2 decomposition. The heterogeneous catalytic Fenton oxidations of organic antibiotic molecules using new NZVI@CD particles have been conducted. Generally, oxidation triggered through the reactivity of NZVI@CD with hydrogen peroxide. The observed and predicted removal efficiencies for AMX and CIP using NZVI@CD catalysts are compared in Table 3 . After analyzing experimental data by Design-Expert software, the reduced quadratic model for AMX removal efficiency (R1) should is developed. Also, the two-factor interaction (2FI) polynomial fitting equation describes the CIP removal efficiency (R2). Both of predicted models in terms of coded values are as follow:
R
1
= 58.41 − 24.91 A − 3.91 B + 1.36 C − 35.69
B
2
+ 19.31
C
2
R
2
= 29.61 − 6.76 A + 6 B + 5.44 C − 1.27 A B − 1.96 A C + 8.39 B C
where A, B, and C are hydrogen peroxide amount (mM), catalyst dosage (mg/l) and reaction time (min), respectively. Depending on the positive or negative sign of hydrogen peroxide amount, catalyst dosage and reaction time in both predicted models, they have favorable or unfavorable effects on the AMX and CIP oxidation process.

The relationship between the process variable and antibiotic removal can be found using a schematic representation of variables effects. Fig. 2 shows the simultaneous interaction of the two process variables on the antibiotic removal. One of the important parameters in designing economical pollutant removal system is the reaction time. The efficiency of as-prepared catalyst for antibiotic removal was examined for the reaction time in the range of 20–60 min. As evident from results, the NZVI@CD catalyst can eliminate high amount of AMX within defined reaction time and for achieving greater CIP degradation, further time is required. The hydrogen peroxide amount was changed from 20 to 60 mM. The stoichiometric H2O2 concentration for complete mineralization of 100 mg/l of AMX (eqn. (2)) and CIP (eqn. (3)) using Fenton reagent is 12.9 and 14.2 mM, respectively.
(2) C16H19N3O5S+47H2O2+Fe (II) →16CO2+54H2O+3HNO3+H2SO4+Fe (II)
(3) C17H18N3O3F+47H2O2→17CO2+54H2O+3HNO3+HF

Based on the selected range for this study, the presence of excess H2O2 at values higher than 20 mM, reduces the production rate of OH• due to oxidizing of Fe2+ to Fe3+ (Shrivastava et al., 2010; Kugelmann et al., 2011). Also, by increasing the amount of H2O2, besides the auto-scavenging reactions, other less oxidation potential radicals may be produced and ultimately when the concentration of OH• radicals are too high these radicals can dimerize to form H2O2 (Arslan-Alaton et al., 2009). Thus these cumulative effects inhibit the removal rate of both antibiotics (De León et al., 2008; Wu et al., 2008). In the studied range of the catalyst from 100 to 1000 mg/l, the removal of both antibiotics was positively correlated with the catalyst dosage. The increase of catalyst amount corresponds to an increase in the number of active sites for catalytic decomposition of H2O2 and enhancement of the OH• formation, therefore, the removal rates increased. It can be concluded that based on the electrocatalytic potential of the NZVI@CD catalyst, the interaction of NZVI@CD catalysts with highly reactive hydroxyl radicals occurs because of the electron-transfer annihilation. Moreover, nano Fe0 encapsulated in biopolymer-based CDs exhibits several beneficial effects such as high surface area and reactivity, high surface-area-to-volume ratios, and fewer tendencies to form micro-sized aggregated structures due to its higher size than bare NZVI. It should be considered that the antibiotic removal efficiencies highly depend on the properties of both the antibiotic and the NZVI@CD catalyst structure at different pH values (Elmolla and Chaudhuri, 2010). As reported by several authors, high amount of AMX degradation under the acidic heterogeneous catalytic Fenton reaction can be assigned to the production of OH• radicals which can oxidize reactive AMX molecule rapidly (Anipsitakis and Dionysiou, 2003). AMX molecule has a very strained ring and also the presence of a highly labile bond that exists between the nitrogen atom and carbonyl in its β-lactam ring gives intensive reactivity to it (García-Reiriz et al., 2007). In the case of CIP, its removal efficiency only reaches 50% after 60 min reaction. The possible explanation may be due to this fact that although the selected pH (acidic condition) is favorable for the heterogeneous catalytic Fenton reaction, it is an unfavorable condition for CIP molecule. At the studied pH (3.5), CIP is mainly in its cationic form and therefore CIP may complex with the ferric ion thus affect the rate of removal (Xiao et al., 2010). Moreover, it has been reported that the anionic species of the fluoroquinolone antibiotics such as CIP, present greater rate constants in comparison to the corresponding zwitterion and cationic species (Márquez et al., 2013). Márquez et al. (2013) found that the second-order rate constant for the reaction between ofloxacin, a quinolone antibiotic, and hydroxyl radicals increases with pH. Therefore, for achieving a higher removal rate of CIP working at higher values of pH is needed.

The statistical importance of both models is evaluated using ANOVA analysis at 95% confidence level and results are summarized in Table 4 . The R2 values for reduced quadratic and 2-FI models were 0.95 and 0.84, respectively and indicate the satisfactory agreement between model predictions and experimental data's. As the p-values for lack-of-fit F-tests were greater than 0.05, it can be concluded that the shortage of the both proposed models for the antibiotic removal is not significant and further validated both models adequacy.

It should be noted that in the case of response R1, both variables B and C in the RSM were not statistically significant but regarding the ANOVA table, their second order effects i.e. B2 and C2 are significant. It can be concluded that the effects of this variable are non-liner and it is difficult to interpret them. To show the high sensitivity of the AMX removal efficiency to both variables B and C the perturbation plot was used and for elucidate the effects of all considered variables on the AMX removal a perturbation plot is presented in Fig. 3 . This figure provides the comparative effects of the variables on AMX removal. A substantial slope or curvature in a variable indicating the high sensitivity of the process response to the change in this variable. As shown in Fig. 3, the steep curvature in B and C curves imply that the AMX removal was very sensitive to both catalyst dosage and reaction time variables.

Finally, because optimization is a necessary tool to have an acceptable compromise between the defined reaction conditions and the optimum process response, the optimization of independent variables for maximum antibiotic removal was carried out using RSM and according to the desirability function approach. The optimized antibiotic removals by taking into account the interaction between processes variables are presented in Fig. 4 (a) and (b). As can be seen in these figures, the best conditions to achieve 90% AMX and 51% CIP removal are 20 mM of H2O2 and 765 mg/l of catalyst in 60 min reaction time. The obtained optimum results were validated by performing experiments and only 2–4% variation was observed between the predicted and actual removal efficiencies. Also, the antibiotic adsorption capabilities of bare NZVI@CD catalyst are explored under the optimal condition. Only the negligible amounts of 11% and 15% of CIP and AMX were removed, respectively. It implies that active OH• radicals are the major oxidant species to cleavage the AMX and CIP organic molecules in the solution and adsorption would not affect antibiotic removal rate. Moreover, the removal efficiency of CIP under optimum operating conditions of the NZVI@CD-mediated Fenton reaction was studied in the pH values of 3.5–9.5 and the maximum removal was obtained in the weak alkaline pH of 9.5. The reason for the highest removal efficiency at this pH can be attributed to the production of hydroxyl radicals with a rate constant of 6.5 × 107 M1 s1 under alkaline conditions (Buxton et al., 1988; Xiong et al., 2014). At this pH, higher than 85% CIP removal was achieved in only 30 min reaction time. These more OH• radicals in which produced under alkaline conditions are highly reactive toward CIP as confirmed by the high second order rate constant of CIP-OH• reaction (Ji et al., 2014).
(4)
C I P + O
H
•
→ i n t e r m e d i a t e s
( k =
( 2.15 ± 0.10 )
×
10
10
M
− 1
s
− 1
)

For determination of the contribution of OH• radicals in the Fenton degradation process using NZVI@CD particles, a classic quenching test with radical scavenger was performed. EtOH was employed to identify the generated OH• radicals by the decomposition of H2O2 over NZVI@CD catalysts during experiments. It is proven that EtOH can quench OH• with the rates of 1.2 × 109–2.8 × 109 (Wang et al., 2015). For this purpose, a defined amount of EtOH was spiked to the reaction mixture. In the presence of scavenger, AMX and CIP removal from the aqueous solution were 14% and 32% compared to 50% and 90% which obtained in the reaction without EtOH, respectively. It can be concluded that the hydroxyl radicals played a dominating role in the AMX and CIP removal during Fenton's oxidation by NZVI@CD particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring mechanism of the reaction between NZVI@CD and H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The possible mechanism of the reaction between synthesized NZVI@CD nanoparticles and H2O2 and ultimately production of hydroxyl radicals are studied using FTIR measurements. The FTIR spectrums of NZVI@CD before and after H2O2 addition are shown in Fig. 5 . Fourier spectrum of the NZVI@CD particles presented several organic functional groups. It is evident that after reaction of H2O2 with NZVI@CD, the peak of NZVI at 624 cm−1 (Malakootian et al., 2017) is disappeared and an increase in intensity and absorbance of both peaks is observed which are related to symmetric Fe-O stretch and vibrations (474 and 671 cm−1) (Liu and Zhang, 2014). It can be concluded that NZVI/CD reacts with H2O2 and after reaction due to oxidation, a mixture of different iron oxides are formed. This means that NZVI is reduced and convert H2O2 to OH• radicals. Also, a sharper band at 1095 cm−1 is observed that corresponds to the ether groups (C-O-C) of the carbohydrate structure. The skeletal body of carbohydrate polymer is opened and the enclosed NZVI is released into the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability test of the NZVI@CD and bare NZVI nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Immediately after preparation, the initial average particle sizes of bare-NZVI and NZVI@CD were measured 237 and 30 nm, respectively. After 3 days, there were slight changes in the average size of the NZVI@CD particles and indicate negligible aggregation among them. As evident from Fig. 6 the size of bare-NZVI particles increased sharply after 72 h. This observation indicates that bare-NZVI particles tended to aggregate with time. Due to the small size of bare NZVI, a higher reactivity and surface energy between them causes the rapid aggregation (Li et al., 2006; Huang et al., 2015). In other experiments, the ability of synthesized NZVI@CD and bare NZVI nanoparticles were examined for antibiotic removal in different time periods at room temperature. For this purpose, the prepared nanoparticles solution were maintained in the ambient air for 7 days and during this time, at optimized reaction conditions (catalyst dose: 765 mg/l, H2O2 amount: 20 mM and time of 60 min), antibiotic oxidation tests were conducted. Initially, it can be seen that the NZVI alone exhibits higher efficiency on AMX and CIP removal in comparison with NZVI@CD. The changes in the amount of hydrogen gas produced after the reaction of the NZVI@CD and bare NZVI nanoparticles with hydrogen peroxide can be seen in Fig. 7 . The results of the removal experiments are presented in Fig. 8 . Although initially, the number of hydroxyl radicals produced and, consequently, the antibiotics removal efficiency by bare NZVI was higher, their efficiencies have declined dramatically over time. After 7 days, the amount of reaction between NZVI and H2O2 is so low that the produced hydrogen gas cannot be seen. As reported by other researchers, the reactivity of NZVI against pollutants (antibiotics) decreases after long-term air/water exposure as a result of the growing of the oxide/hydroxide shell accompanying with particles passivation (Liu et al., 2007; Kim et al., 2012; Xie and Cwiertny, 2012; Woo et al., 2014). From the oxidation experiments, it can be concluded that the encapsulation of NZVI to the CDs improved its stability. According to the FTIR data and other observations of the removal tests over time, it can be concluded that the carbon dots have been able to safely keep zero-valent iron nanocrystals against oxidation and slowly release it into the aqueous medium over time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Fenton reaction in real wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the effect of the water matrix on the efficiency of the Fenton process using NZVI@CD and hydrogen peroxide for antibiotic removal, additional experiments were performed. Under defined optimum process conditions ([H2O2] of 20 mM, time of 60 min and amount of catalyst of 765 mg/l), experiments were conducted using a hospital effluent. Wastewater (WW) from the Farabi hospital of the Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences was collected. A two-step treatment system was established to treat the WW in this hospital. Firstly the raw hospital WW was passed through an activated sludge treatment plant followed by a bio-filter process. The main parameters of the raw WW, WW after activated sludge plant and WW after bio-filter treatment (Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD), Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN), NH4-N, NO2-N and NO3-N, Turbidity, Total Phosphate (TP), Total Suspended Solids (TSS), Electro-Conductivity (EC), pH) were reported in Table 5 .

The WW after bio-filter (the sample after a two-step treatment) was used as a real sample to carry out the Fenton process. The WW was spiked with 100 mg/l of CIP and AMX. In the case of real WW, a lower removal efficiency of AMX and CIP was observed than those in aqueous solution. The removal efficiencies of AMX and CIP in real WW achieving 71 and 34% after 60 min reaction time, respectively. The differences were related to the composition of real WW which contain different organic and inorganic compounds. It should be noted that the number of free radicals in both aqueous solution and real WW was constant but the organic load in real WW needs more oxidative agents for more AMX and CIP removal. Considering the relatively low COD and TSS contents in the real WW, antibiotics removal efficiencies were decrease which is due to the consumption of hydroxyl radical in side reactions.

An attempt has been made to designing new NZVI composite by encapsulating in biopolymer based carbon dots in order to increase its stability/utilization ratio and decrease agglomeration/fast oxidation. In a novel and simple hydrothermal synthesize method, the synthesis and stabilization of the Fe0 nanoparticles are carried out simultaneously and in one step. The carbon dots obtained from carbonization of Gum Tragacanth reduce iron salt and generate Fe0, and act as a capping agent for Fe0. To examine the oxidation ability of the new composite, the NZVI@CD catalytic-mediated Fenton oxidation of two widely used antibiotics, AMX and CIP were performed. The removal of AMX by NZVI@CD catalysts in the presence of H2O2 was more efficient than that of CIP. It is probably due to more reactivity of AMX molecule structure and the tendency of CIP to form a complex with ferric ions in the considered conditions. Also, the low reactivity of protonated amines (a cationic form of CIP) towards hydroxyl radicals in the acidic pH is one of the possible explanations for lower CIP removal. According to the FTIR results and other observations of the removal tests over time, it can be concluded that the encapsulation of NZVI to the CDs improved its stability and the carbon dots have been able to safely keep zero-valent iron particles against oxidation and slowly release it into the aqueous medium over time. Finally, it should be noted that any green production is accompanying with the lowering of toxic chemicals and reducing the waste. Moreover, given the reduction of water resources and the increase in the global population, it is necessary to develop optimal refinement processes to return wastewater to the consumption cycle. Also, the discharge of antibiotics to the environment can cause major hazards to human health and in the other hand developing some microbial resistance that which could have devastating social and economic effects on human societies. These reasons confirm the importance of the interrelationship between the obtained results and sustainable development/cleaner production.
None.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Iran National Science Foundation, Iran (Grant Number: 95014379) and Research Council of Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Iran (Grant Number: 96349) for their financial support.
